1: Rhētor Tertullos tis ('a certain orator named Tertullus'): Tertullus is the first professional advocate (rhētor) explicitly named in Acts. His name is Latin — suggesting a Roman Jew, a Romanized Jew, or a Gentile hired for his rhetorical expertise. The high priest's decision to hire professional legal counsel reflects the seriousness of the case and the inadequacy of the Sanhedrin's own members to argue before a Roman governor in the forensic rhetorical style that Felix would expect. Professional orators trained in Latin and Greek forensic rhetoric were available for hire throughout the empire.
2: Pollēs eirēnēs tynchanontes dia sou kai katorthōmatōn ginomenōn tō ethnei toutō dia tēs sēs pronoias ('we enjoy much peace through you and reforms in this nation through your foresight'): the captatio benevolentiae (capture of goodwill) was a required opening in Roman forensic oratory — complimenting the judge on virtues relevant to the case. Tertullus's praise of Felix for 'peace' is historically ironic: Felix's tenure (52-60 CE) was characterized by multiple violent suppressions of social movements, including the Egyptian prophet's movement (Acts 21:38) and the first significant Zealot uprisings. Josephus (Wars 2.13; Ant. 20.8.5) describes Felix as cruel and rapacious. The flattery is formulaic, not factual — and both Tertullus and Felix know it.
3: Pantē te kai pantachou apodechometha, kratiste Phēlix, meta pasēs eucharistias ('everywhere and in every way, most excellent Felix, we acknowledge this with all gratitude'): the accumulated superlatives — 'everywhere,' 'in every way,' 'most excellent,' 'all gratitude' — pile up in conventional forensic flattery. Roman forensic oratory required such openings; courts expected them; skilled judges measured speeches by their argumentative quality rather than by their compliments. The density of the flattery signals its conventional, not sincere, character — a performance both parties recognized as such.
4: Kleptō se epi pleion ('I do not want to take up too much of your time'): the brevitas topos — claiming brevity before a speech — was a standard rhetorical device (Latin praeteritio). By promising brevity, the orator signals confidence in the case's strength and respect for the judge's time, both of which cultivate goodwill. Tertullus's actual speech is relatively brief; his promise of brevity is kept more consistently than most ancient orators who invoke the convention.
5: Loimos ('a plague/pestilence'): a stock insult in ancient rhetoric for political troublemakers and social agitators — Demosthenes had called Aeschines a loimos. Prōtostatēs ('ringleader/front-rank man'): a military term for the man in the front rank, used here metaphorically for the leader of a disruptive movement. Hairesis ('sect'): the first application of this term to Christians in Acts. In Paul's letters hairesis means 'faction, divisive party' (1 Corinthians 11:19; Galatians 5:20); Tertullus uses it to characterize Christianity as a schismatic faction within Judaism — undermining its claim to be the legitimate fulfillment of Israel's heritage.
6: Ton hieron epeiresen bebēlōsai ('he tried to desecrate the temple'): the charge has been downgraded from the original accusation in Acts 21:28 — where the crowd alleged Paul brought Trophimus the Ephesian into the inner courts (an offense punishable by death under both Jewish and Roman law, confirmed by Josephus Wars 6.2.4). By the time of the formal charge, the accusers have shifted to 'tried to desecrate' — a vaguer, attempted offense. The weakening of the specific charge is itself evidence that the original accusation could not be proven.
7: The verse in some manuscripts adds that Lysias 'with great violence took him from our hands, ordering his accusers to come before you' — a Western text expansion likely added to explain the transition from Acts 24:6 to Acts 24:8. Most critical editions bracket or omit it. Its insertion shows ancient readers sensed a gap in the speech's logic that needed bridging; Luke's original text apparently moved from the temple-desecration charge directly to 'examine him yourself' without this editorial addition.
8: Para hou dynēsē autos anakrinas ('from whom you will be able, by examining him yourself'): Tertullus refers the decisive evidence to Paul himself — an unusual rhetorical confidence. He assumes Felix's examination will confirm the charges. The confidence may be rhetorical bluff, or it may reflect the accusers' expectation that Felix would accept their characterization of Paul as a troublemaker and act accordingly without rigorous investigation. Paul's counter-speech will demolish this expectation by meeting every charge with specific facts.
9: Synepethento de kai hoi Ioudaioi ('the Jews also joined in the accusation'): the congregation of accusers — high priest, delegation of elders, professional orator, and supporting Jewish witnesses — provides collective testimony. In Roman law, multiple witnesses were important; the concurrence appears overwhelming. Paul's response must counter not just the legal argument but the social weight of an entire institutional leadership testifying against him.
10: Ek pollōn etōn onta se kritēn tō ethnei toutō epistamenos ('knowing that for many years you have been a judge over this nation'): Paul's captatio benevolentiae is noticeably shorter and more measured than Tertullus's — he compliments Felix's experience rather than his beneficence. Judicial experience is a neutral-positive quality: it implies Felix knows the law, is not easily fooled, and can distinguish genuine evidence from accusation. The appeal to competence rather than generosity is a more restrained and arguably more effective form of judicial appeal.
11: Ou pleiōn eisin moi hēmerai dōdeka ('it is not more than twelve days since I went up to worship'): the chronological argument is precise and falsifiable — twelve days is incompatible with the charge of organizing widespread agitation. Paul counts the days from his arrival in Jerusalem (Acts 21:15-17 — three days before meeting James) to his arrest. The brevity of the visit, verifiable by the Sanhedrin members themselves, directly undercuts Tertullus's characterization of Paul as a systematic troublemaker 'all over the world.'
12: Oute en tō hierō heuron me pros tina dialegomonon ('they did not find me arguing with anyone at the temple'): Paul's triple denial — no arguing at the temple, no stirring up crowds in the synagogues, no creating disturbances anywhere in the city — directly addresses Tertullus's three-part charge (pestilence/troublemaker, leader of a sect, temple desecrator). The legal form mirrors the accusation's structure, a classical rhetorical technique for rebuttal called refutatio.
13: Oude parastēsai dynantai soi peri hōn nyn katēgorousin mou ('nor can they prove to you the charges they are now bringing against me'): the burden-of-proof challenge — paristēmi (prove, demonstrate) is a legal term — places the evidentiary burden where Roman law placed it: on the accusers. Paul does not merely deny; he challenges the accusers to produce verifiable evidence. The challenge will go unanswered — the real witnesses (the Asian Jews who made the original accusation) are absent (Acts 24:19).
14: Homotogō de touto soi hoti kata tēn hodon ēn legousin hairesin ('I admit this to you: according to the Way which they call a sect'): Paul's counter-labeling reappropriates Tertullus's hairesis (Acts 24:5) — he accepts the label ('which they call a sect') while stripping it of pejorative force. The Way is not a destructive faction but the fulfillment of the ancestral faith. 'Believing all things in the Law and the Prophets' (pisteuōn pasin tois kata ton nomon kai en tois prophētais gegrammēnois) plants Paul firmly within Israel's canonical tradition — Christianity is not apostasy but fulfillment.
15: Anastasin mellein esesthai dikaiōn te kai adikōn ('there will be a resurrection of both the righteous and the wicked'): the universal resurrection — of both righteous and wicked, for judgment — draws from Daniel 12:2, the most explicit OT statement of bodily resurrection. The inclusion of the wicked's resurrection distinguishes this from merely a happy afterlife — the resurrection is for accountability, not only for reward. Paul presents this hope as shared with the Pharisees in the Sanhedrin delegation (Acts 24:15, 'which these men themselves hold').
16: Aproskopon syneidēsin echein pros ton theon kai tous anthrōpous dia pantos ('to maintain a clear conscience before God and man always'): aproskopon (not stumbling, causing no offense) + syneidēsis (conscience) is the same vocabulary cluster Paul uses in his opening statement to the Sanhedrin (Acts 23:1). The dual accountability — to God and to the human community — frames Paul's moral life as transparently public. This is Paul's consistent self-presentation in Acts: his behavior is verifiable, his conscience is clean, and he invites scrutiny.
17: Dia etōn pleionōn eleēmosynas poiēsōn eis to ethnos mou kai prosphoras ('after several years, bringing charitable gifts and offerings to my nation'): Luke's only reference to Paul's Jerusalem collection — the major organizational project of Paul's third missionary journey that dominates 2 Cor 8-9, Romans 15:25-28, and 1 Corinthians 16:1-4. The collection was Paul's concrete gesture of Gentile-church solidarity with the Jerusalem community. Luke's restraint in mentioning it (one clause, not elaborated) is itself a historical datum — he chooses to include it as evidence of Paul's good-faith relationship with Jerusalem, not as a theological showcase of inter-church generosity.
18: Hēgnismenon en tō hierō ('having completed purification in the temple'): Paul's temple presence was in the context of fulfilled Nazirite vows — the most Jewish possible activity, not desecration. He was in a state of ritual purity (hagnismenon), completing socially approved vow-rituals, when the accusation arose. The original charge of bringing Gentiles into the inner courts (Acts 21:28) collapses against the actual circumstances of his visit: he was not bringing Gentiles in but completing Jewish purification acts while in a state of purity himself.
19: Hous edei epi sou pareinai kai katēgorein ei ti echoien pros eme ('who ought to be present before you and make an accusation if they have anything against me'): the absent-witnesses argument is Paul's strongest legal point. Roman law required accusers to appear in person — the principle confirmed by Trajan's rescript to Pliny (Ep. 10.97, 'It is not consistent with justice to condemn without accusers present') and implicitly in Lysias's letter (Acts 23:30, 'ordering his accusers to present their case before you'). The Asian Jews who originally made the charge are not present; their absence is a procedural deficiency that should end the case under Roman law.
20: E autoi houtoi eipatōsan ti heuron adikēma ('or let these men here state what crime they found in me'): Paul invites the present accusers — those who were at the Sanhedrin hearing — to state their case directly. The only specific charge they can offer is the theological statement about resurrection (Acts 24:21). Everything else is allegation without witnesses. Paul's advocacy is tight and specific: he forces the accusers to either produce evidence or narrow to the actual substantive disagreement, which is theological rather than criminal.
21: Peri anastaseōs nekrōn egō krinomai sēmeron eph' hymōn ('it is concerning the resurrection of the dead that I am on trial today before you'): Paul's self-summary cuts through all the political and procedural complexity to the theological core. The resurrection is the real question; everything else is obscuring it. The statement is simultaneously a legal argument (there is no criminal charge, only a theological dispute) and a theological proclamation (the resurrection is the gospel's center). Paul uses the trial as a platform for proclamation, not merely as a defense.
22: Akribēsteron eidōs ta peri tēs hodou ('knowing the Way more accurately'): Felix's knowledge of the Way — whether through Drusilla (Acts 24:24), through administrative experience in Judea, or through other sources — is historically plausible. Felix had governed Judea for years (52-60 CE) and would have had extensive contact with the Jewish community and its internal movements. Anebaleato autous ('he adjourned the proceedings'): the formal procedural postponement — neither conviction nor acquittal. Felix's deferral is a judicial non-decision made from political calculation: he cannot convict Paul without evidence, but releasing him would alienate the Jewish leadership he needed for political stability.
23: Echein te anesin ('to have some freedom/relief'): Paul's custody is custodia libera (free custody) — supervised but not confined. Mēdena kōlyein tōn idiōn autou hypēretein autō ('no one to prevent his own people from serving him'): the apostolic community could visit, bring resources, and maintain relationship. This arrangement explains how Paul maintained his ministry connections during the Caesarean imprisonment — possibly writing or dictating letters during this period, with Philippians, Philemon, and Colossians among the candidates proposed by various scholars.
24: Drusilla, gynaiki ousē Ioudaia ('Drusilla, who was Jewish'): Drusilla was the youngest daughter of Herod Agrippa I (who died in Acts 12:23 — the same man whose death Luke records) and the sister of Agrippa II (who appears in Acts 25-26). She had left her first husband Azizus of Emesa — who had converted to Judaism to marry her — to marry Felix, a Gentile, violating Jewish law on both counts (marriage to an uncircumcised Gentile; abandonment of her first husband). Her Jewish identity made her particularly well-positioned to understand Paul's arguments; her personal history made Paul's speech about righteousness and self-control immediately relevant (Acts 24:25).
25: Peri dikaiosynēs kai enkrateias kai tou krimatos tou mellontos ('about righteousness, self-control, and the coming judgment'): the three themes are precisely calibrated to Felix and Drusilla's situation. Dikaiosynē (righteousness) before a governor who administered justice corruptly; enkrateia (self-control) to a man who had induced a married woman to leave her husband; krima mellon (the coming judgment) to a man who was misusing his judicial power. Emphobos genomenos ('becoming afraid'): Felix's fear is the recognition of personal moral exposure — the first response of conscience to the gospel's claims. That he sends Paul away rather than responding further shows the fear was insufficient to overcome self-interest.
26: Chrēmata dothēsetai autō hypo tou Paulou ('money would be given to him by Paul'): Felix's hope for a bribe reflects the corruption Josephus attests (Ant. 20.8.5; Wars 2.13.2 — 'he exercised the authority of a king with the mentality of a slave'). His frequent conversations with Paul (pyknōteron metepempto auton kai hōmilei autō, 'he sent for him frequently and conversed with him') reveal mixed motives: some genuine interest (perhaps stimulated by Drusilla) and mercenary calculation. The bribe-expectation explains the frequency of conversations: each was an opportunity for Paul to make an offer. Paul's willingness to speak repeatedly to Felix despite the bribe-motivation is his sustained witness to a powerful, sinful audience.
27: Dietias de plērōtheis ... katēleipen ton Paulon dedemenon charin katathesthai tois Ioudaiois ('after two years Felix left Paul imprisoned to do a favor to the Jews'): Felix was recalled to Rome around AD 59-60 — Josephus records that he was accused by the Jews of Caesarea and only avoided punishment through the intercession of his brother Pallas at court. His final act — leaving Paul imprisoned as a gift to the Jewish leadership — was a transparently political decision: sacrificing a prisoner's legal rights to maintain political relationships at the end of a troubled tenure. The two years between Paul's arrival in Caesarea and Festus's appointment is the span covered by Acts 23:23-24:27.
